coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of high photoluminescence nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots from grass via a simple hydrothermal method for removing organic and inorganic water pollutions
coredata.dc:creator: Sabet, Mohammad
Mahdavi, Kamran
coredata.dc:description: In this work, highly photoluminescence nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQD) were synthesized via a simple hydrothermal method from a very low cost and green material. Different analysis were used to approve synthesis of the quantum dots such as X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD), energy dispersive X-ray analysis (EDAX) and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). The morphology of the product was investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images. In addition, the surface topography was studied by atomic force microscopy (AFM) and it was found the product has tiny and uniform particles. The photoluminescence (PL) analysis was served to study the photoluminescence intensity and it was found the product has high photoluminescence intensity. To investigate the photocatalytic activity of the product, five dyes namely Acid Blue, Acid Red, Eosin Y, Eriochrome Black T, Methyl orange and Methylene blue were decomposed under radiation. Surface activity of the product was evaluated by adsorption Cd2+ and Pb2+ from the water and the results showed the carbon dots can remove these two heavy metal ions from the water with 37% and 75%, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Nitrogen
Grass
Hydrothermal
Photocatalyst
Heavy Metal Ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots
Dye solution preparation for photocatalytic activity study

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM image, (b) proceeded SEM image by Image J software and (c) particle size histogram of the synthesized carbon quantum dots.
(a) UV–Vis spectra and b) (ahv)2 versus hv curve.
Degradation of Acid Blue in presence of N-CQD.
Degradation of Acid Red in presence of N-CQD.
Degradation of Eosin Y in presence of N-CQD.
Degradation of Methylene Blue in presence of N-CQD.
Degradation of ECBT in presence of N-CQD.
Degradation of Methyl Orange in presence of N-CQD.
The effects of (a) pH, (b) contact time and (c, d) dye concentration on the degradation rate.
Removing of Cd2+ from the water in presence of N-CQD.
Removing of Pb2+ from the water in presence of N-CQD.
Schematic of degradation of the selected dyes in presence of N-CQD under irradiation.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of high photoluminescence nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots from grass via a simple hydrothermal method for removing organic and inorganic water pollutions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Vali-e-Asr University of Rafsanjan, Rafsanjan, PO Box: 77176, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, highly photoluminescence nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQD) were synthesized via a simple hydrothermal method from a very low cost and green material. Different analysis were used to approve synthesis of the quantum dots such as X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD), energy dispersive X-ray analysis (EDAX) and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). The morphology of the product was investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images. In addition, the surface topography was studied by atomic force microscopy (AFM) and it was found the product has tiny and uniform particles. The photoluminescence (PL) analysis was served to study the photoluminescence intensity and it was found the product has high photoluminescence intensity. To investigate the photocatalytic activity of the product, five dyes namely Acid Blue, Acid Red, Eosin Y, Eriochrome Black T, Methyl orange and Methylene blue were decomposed under radiation. Surface activity of the product was evaluated by adsorption Cd2+ and Pb2+ from the water and the results showed the carbon dots can remove these two heavy metal ions from the water with 37% and 75%, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent nanomaterials have generated many applications in sensing [1], imaging [2], and optoelectronic devices [3] because of their unique optical properties. Quantum dots materials are very strong metal ions detector due to their significant optical sensing behavior and also size-tunable emission profiles [4]. But due to they are very toxic even at a low value, using them has been limited [5]. Carbon quantum dots are promising materials using instead of traditional toxic quantum dots. They have significant luminescence properties [6] that can be used in the biosensors, bioimaging and light-emitting diode, and have high biocompatibility [7–9]. The other significant properties of these nanomaterials are that they are water soluble and can be easy functionalized [10–12]. Until now, different methods were used to the synthesis of carbon quantum dots such as electrochemical oxidation, ultrasonic method, pyrolysis of glycerol, microwave method, thermal carbonization of molecules, atmospheric plasma-based synthesis [13–17]. Among different methods, hydrothermal carbonization is an effective approach to the synthesis of carbon quantum dots due to several reasons. In the hydrothermal method, we used water that leads to the creation of carbon quantum dots in a mild experimental condition [18]. In addition, hydrothermal method use from cheap instruments. Also by using this method, we can easily synthesis such quantum dots from biowaste materials. Synthesis of nanomaterials from green materials, nontoxic chemicals, renewable materials and green solvents attract significant consideration with researchers [19]. Until now many researchers synthesis of carbon quantum dots from green materials. For example Chunduri and et al used coconut husk to synthesis of CQDs from hydrothermal method and evaluated them for antioxidant cytotoxic activity [20]. Tyagi and coworkers synthesized CQDs from lemon peel waste and used them for sensing and photocatalysis [21]. Carbon quantum dots were synthesized by Tripathi and et al from pyrolysis of kidney bean and were served for cell imaging [22]. Bano and et al used Tamarindus Indica to synthesis of fluorescent carbon quantum dots for the detection of mercury (II) and glutathione [23]. Carbon quantum dots were also synthesized by D’souza and coworkers with carrot for using in drug delivery [24].Recently, the study of removing of the soil and water pollutions have attracted many attentions by the researchers [25,26]. Heavy metal ion pollution has become a critical worldwide issue for years due to the severe risks in human health and the environment [27]. In this experimental work, we synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots from grass with the help of a simple hydrothermal method. Different analysis were used to approve synthesis of the quantum dots and study their particle sizes such as XRD, EDS, FT-IR, SEM, and TEM. Different dyes were degradation in presence of the synthesized quantum dots to investigate their photocatalytic activity and potential rule to remove pollutants from the drinking water. Also, their ability to remove heavy metal ions from the water was examined by remove of cadmium and lead ions from the water. The results showed the synthesized product has significant photocatalytic activity and surface adsorption and it can be used in removing organic and inorganic pollutions from the drinking water. Based on our knowledge, this is the first work in the synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots from grass and using them to remove pollutions from the water.

In this experimental work, just two materials were used to synthesis of high yield nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots: grass and water. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were detected on Shimadzu Varian 4300 spectrophotometer in KBr pellets. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded by a Panalytical -X’pertpro, X-ray diffractometer using Ni-filtered Cu Ka radiation. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image was applied on TESCAN equipped with an energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy. The EDX analysis with 20 kV accelerated voltage was applied. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was achieved on a Zeiss transmission electron microscope with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. UV–Vis spectra were recorded using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) image was obtained with Multi-Mode atomic force microscope (Ara Research Company, Iran).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: A certain amount of grass was completely grinned in a mortar and poured in 100 ml of distilled water in a beaker. After that, the beaker was inserted in an autoclave and heated two hours at 180 °C. The obtained powder was washed with distilled water and dried at 80 °C for 24 h. Scheme. 1 shows experimental procedure schematic.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dye solution preparation for photocatalytic activity study
ce:section.ce:para: To study the photocatalytic activity of the product, 0.003 g of Acid Blue, 0.001 g of Acid Red, 0.04 g of Methyl Orange, 0.003 g of Eriochrome Black T, 0.015 g of Methylene Blue, and 0.007 g of Eosin Y were dissolved in 50 ml of water, separately and were decomposed in presence of 0.3 g of N-CQD.
Fig. 1 shows XRD pattern of the product. As can be seen, it has an amorphous structure with a broad peak at 2θ = 22° that is clearly related to the amorphous nature of C-dots.

The chemical composition of the product was examined by EDS analysis. The results are shown in Fig. 2 . It can be seen that the product is mainly composed of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen elements with 43.62, 43.43 and 12.95 atomic percent, respectively. As can be seen in the EDS results, the atomic percent of oxygen is equal to the carbon atomic percent. The main reason for the presence of oxygen element in the product is related to the hydroxyl and carboxylic group on the product surface. In addition, due to huge surface of the product, the most carbons are located at the surface and hence they are equal to the oxygens bonded to the carbons surfaces. The presence of nitrogen in the product surfaces can be attributed to the nitrogens bonded to the carbon atoms in the bulk and surface of the product.

Surface functional groups on the CQDs were identified by FT-IR spectra (Fig. 3 ). The peak located at 3430 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of OH group [28] that is related to the OH bonded to the carbon surface and also the adsorbed water on the product surfaces. The peak at 2922 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibration of CH [28]. The peaks located at 1630, 1385 and 1050 cm−1 are related to the vibrational absorption of CO, CO (carboxy) and CO (alkoxy), respectively that is similar to the previous work results [29,30]. In addition the band located at 1440 cm−1 can be assigned to the bending vibrations of NH group [31]. It can be concluded that the product surface is covered by lots of amino and hydroxyl groups.

To study the product size and morphology, SEM images were obtained (Fig. 4 a). The results showed the product is composed of very tiny particles. The particles were so small that aggregated together and larger chunk like structures were achieved. In fact, due to very small size of the carbon particles, they have high surface energy that lead to aggregation of them. To show how small the particles are, the SEM image was proceed by image J software. As shown in Fig. 4b, it can be seen that the product is composed of the particles smaller than 10 nm. So we can name the product as quantum dots. In addition, from the histogram curve, it can be observed that the most particles are smaller than 10 nm in diameter. So it can be concluded that the product has huge surface area due to their small particles. We used from this ability to remove heavy metal ions and decompose of some organic and inorganic pigments from the aqueous solution explained in the next sections.
To approve SEM image result, TEM analysis was served (Fig. 5 ) and it was found the product is composed of very tiny particles.
The surface topography and roughness were studied by AFM image. As shown in Fig. 6 , it can be observed that the surface of product is very uniform and the particles aggregated together uniformly.

The optical properties of the product was examined by UV–Vis spectroscopy (Fig. 7 a). It can be seen that the product can absorb light under ultraviolet and visible radiation. The band gap of synthesized N-CQD was calculated from UV–Vis spectra by extrapolation of (ahv)2 versus hv curve (Fig. 7b). The result showed the product has 2.5 eV band gap that is in good agreement with PL spectra result. It can be concluded that the product can be excited under both ultra violet and visible radiation.

The photoluminescence properties of the as synthesized product were studied. The results showed the product has high photoluminsence intensity that can be attributed to the several reasons similar to the previous works [32–34]. As shown in PL spectra (Fig. 8 ), when N-CQD was excited at 490 nm shows an emission in the visible range (640 nm) with high intensity. This emission can be origin from carbon particle size, the availability of sp2 sites, the aromatic conjugate structure and the structure defect [35].

In this experimental work, a simple green synthesis method was served to the fabrication of highly photoluminescent carbon quantum dots from grass with high yield. The different analysis was used to approve synthesis of these quantum dot materials. XRD analysis showed a broad amorphous peak at 22.6° which completely approved the synthesis of N-CQD. EDS analysis showed in addition of carbon, there were nitrogen elements in the product confirmed the synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots. Also, the results showed beside the carbon and nitrogen, the oxygen element is present in the structure that is mainly due to the presence of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the carbon dots surfaces. The surface groups of the product were studied by FT-IR analysis and the results showed the presence of OH, CO, CO and NH functional groups in the product structure. The particle size was examined by SEM and TEM images and it was observed the product is composed of very tiny particles with less than 10 nm in diameter. AFM analysis showed the particles in the product are arranged uniformly. The band gap of the product was calculated from UV–Vis spectra to 2.5 eV and it was found the product can be excited under ultraviolet and visible radiation. From the PL spectra, it was found the product has high photoluminescence intensity that is due to several factors such as carbon particles size, the availability of sp2 sites, the aromatic conjugate structure and the structural defect. Photocatalytic activity of the product was examined by degradation of Acid Blue, Acid Red, Eosin Y, Eriochrome Black T, Methyl orange and Methylene blue dyes. The results showed the product is active both under visible and ultraviolet radiation indicating the high photocatalytic activity of the product. To study the surface adsorption activity of the product, Cd2+ and Pb2+ were removed from the water in presence of N-CQD as a catalyst. The results showed the synthesized carbon dots could remove Pb2+ and Cd2+ with 75% and 37% respectively that is mainly due to very tiny particles existed in the product. In fact, due to the small size particles of the product, it has large surface area and can adsorb the heavy metal ions from the water. It can be concluded the synthesized N-CQD has highly active in both photocatalytic and surface adsorption and can be used to remove both organic and heavy metal pollutions from the drinking water.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are grateful to the Vali-e-Asr University of Rafsanjan for supporting this work. We also thank Dr. Masoud Karimipour (Associate Professor, Department of Physics, Vali-e-Asr University of Rafsanjan) for AFM measurement that improved our paper quality.
